content:
Our agent hallucinated a refund.

We had built a support bot on LangChain to handle order issues.
It worked in staging.
It failed on day 3 in production.

One user asked for a partial refund on a $48 order.
The agent replied with confidence, approved $120, and wrote a fake policy citation.
Our logs showed 17 similar attempts over the next hour.

We first blamed the model.
Then we tried a bigger model, tighter prompts, and a second verification step.

None of it fixed the core issue.

The real problem was simpler:
the agent had write access before it had proof.

It could read the order system, reason about the case, and act.
But there was no hard boundary between suggestion and execution.
One bad tool call was enough.

We changed one thing:
the agent could draft a refund request, but Stripe approvals moved behind a human gate.

The next week, refund errors dropped to zero.

My lesson from that outage:
AI agents are not broken because they think poorly.
They break when we let them act with more confidence than our systems can justify.

Where do you draw the line between agent autonomy and human approval?

Simple architecture beats clever architecture.

I learned that the hard way on a backend rewrite.

We had 14 services, gRPC between teams, and a beautiful diagram in Miro.
It looked like a senior engineer had painted it.

Then one small change took 11 days to ship.

The bottleneck wasn’t code.
It was coordination, versioning, and everyone being afraid to touch the seams.

We cut it down to 3 services and moved two workflows back into a single Rails app.
Not because microservices are bad.
Because our team was too small for the shape we’d built.

Deploys dropped from 6 steps to 1.
On-call pages fell by 40% in the next month.

The contrarian part:
architecture is not a trophy for maturity.
It’s a cost you pay every day.

The strongest argument for complexity is scale.
Sometimes that’s true.

But most teams I’ve seen don’t need more boundaries.
They need fewer places to fail.

What architecture choice looked smart on paper and slowed you down in practice?

We shipped a broken migration.

It touched 18,000 customer records in one night.

The script looked fine in staging.
In production, one missing WHERE clause started rewriting the wrong rows.

Support lit up at 2:13 a.m.
Finance froze refunds.
Engineering spent 11 hours restoring backups from Postgres and re-running jobs by hand.

The cost was not just the incident.
We lost 3 days of roadmap time and a customer who had trusted us with their billing data.

The mistake wasn’t “moving too fast.”
It was shipping a change that could not prove it was safe before it touched real data.

That one still follows me.

Now, before any migration goes live, I want a dry run, a row count, and a rollback plan that a tired person can execute at 2 a.m.

The lesson: if a change can corrupt trust, the burden is on the code to prove it deserves prod.

What’s the worst production mistake that changed how you ship?

I stopped trusting vibe checks.

We were building an AI support bot, and the real problem was simple: how do we tell if it’s actually helping people, or just sounding confident?

That meant comparing two eval tools: LangSmith, the tracing and testing tool from LangChain, and OpenAI Evals, OpenAI’s benchmark runner.

LangSmith was faster to set up.
We had traces on the same day, and could see exactly where answers drifted across 200 customer tickets.

OpenAI Evals was stricter.
It took longer to wire in, but it forced us to write clean test cases and score the model the same way every run.

After 2 weeks, the winner was clear.

LangSmith helped us debug.
OpenAI Evals helped us decide.

When we needed one source of truth for whether a change was better or worse, OpenAI Evals won.
It was less pretty, but more honest.

The lesson: use the tool that makes bad news obvious.
Pretty dashboards are nice.
Repeatable scores keep you from fooling yourself.

Which eval tool has saved you from a false win?

We shipped the dashboard again.

It’s a React app for small ops teams who live in spreadsheets all day.

This week I rebuilt the slowest screen, the one that opens before the morning standup and decides whether people trust the product.

I cut the first load from 4.8 seconds to 1.9.

The change was boring in the best way.

I split one huge React component into smaller pieces, moved the heaviest chart behind a lazy load, and swapped a couple of “just render everything” habits for plain old loading states.

The weird part: the app looked almost the same.

But the behavior changed fast.

Support tickets about “the page froze” dropped from 11 last week to 3 this week.

What’s still broken?

Our filter bar is still doing too much work on every keystroke, and on older laptops you can feel it.

That’s the part I’m fixing next.

The lesson: users don’t care how elegant your React code feels.

They care about whether the screen appears before they give up.

What’s the slowest screen you’ve ever had to make feel fast?

MCP looked simple.

We were wiring an AI assistant to our internal tools.
The goal was basic: let it read tickets, check docs, and draft replies without a human copy-pasting everything.

The decision was where to keep the context.
MCP, the model context protocol, is the standard way a bot can ask for tools and data.
Context engineering is how you decide what the model actually sees.

The tradeoff was speed versus safety.

If we sent everything, the assistant felt smarter.
It also got slower, cost more, and started mixing old details with fresh ones.
If we sent too little, it missed the one line in a ticket that changed the answer.

We chose narrow context plus better retrieval.
Retrieval means fetching only the most relevant info.
That cut average response time from 8 seconds to 2.3 seconds, and the model stopped “helpfully” using stale notes.

The constraint most people miss is attention, not capability.
A model can only reason well about what fits in front of it.

This tradeoff does not hold when the task is exploratory, like research or debugging a messy incident.
Then wider context can be worth the noise.

What do you optimize first when wiring an AI to real tools: breadth or precision?

My metrics post embarrassed me.

I was building a SaaS dashboard for founders.
It tracked signups, trials, and paid conversions.
Then I shared weekly numbers publicly, and the story got ugly.

The traffic looked healthy.
The pipeline looked alive.
But 82% of signups never touched the product again.

I blamed the funnel at first.
So I rewired the onboarding, changed the email sequence, and added a mixpanel event tracker — a tool that logs user actions — to every screen.

Nothing moved.

The real problem was simpler.
I had been measuring curiosity, not commitment.
A blog reader clicking a pricing page made the dashboard look good.
A real customer opening the app twice told the truth.

That was the postmortem:
my build-in-public metrics were rewarding vanity, not traction.

The lesson I keep coming back to is this:
if a metric makes you feel busy instead of honest, it is probably lying to you.

What metric have you seen look impressive while hiding the real story?

Next.js is overrated in prod.

The dominant story is simple:
use Next.js, ship faster, let the framework handle the hard parts.

That sounds right until your app gets real traffic.
Then you start caring about cache behavior, server costs, and pages that look fine in dev but feel sticky in production.

We learned this on a B2B app with about 40,000 monthly visits.
A single product page was taking 1.8 seconds to become useful because of server-side rendering on every request.
Not broken.
Just expensive in the wrong way.

We moved that page to static generation plus a small client fetch.
Same design.
Same copy.
Same users.

The page dropped under 400 ms.
Our AWS bill fell by 27% that month.

My contrarian take:
Next.js is great for speed of building, not always speed of running.

The strongest counterargument is real:
for teams with small apps, tight deadlines, and mixed frontend skills, Next.js is still one of the best defaults.
I agree.

But once traffic, latency, and cloud cost matter, the framework stops being the answer and starts being the tradeoff.

The lesson:
pick your stack for what it will cost you at scale, not how pleasant it feels on day one.

What’s your least favorite production tradeoff with Next.js?

Our agent sent 47 wrong emails.

We were building a support assistant to answer customer tickets and save the team time.
It had access to billing notes, order history, and our help docs.
One bad reply could confuse a real customer and create more work for support.

The bug was not the model.
It was trust.

We let the agent draft an email, then only checked if the text sounded polite.
It sounded great.
It also invented a shipping policy that did not exist.

The fix was boring, and it worked.
We made the agent quote the exact source line for every factual claim.
No source, no send.

After that, the false answers dropped fast.
In one week, the error rate fell from 18% to 2%.

The lesson: if an agent can act on the world, it needs proof, not confidence.

Where do you draw the line between letting an agent speak freely and making it show its work?

TypeScript made two lies

We were building a checkout flow, and the bug was simple: cart data said one thing, the API expected another.

Two tools could have caught it before prod: TypeScript and Zod, the runtime shape checker.

TypeScript is great at compile time, which means it helps while you’re writing code.

Zod checks at runtime, which means it verifies the data after it arrives from an API, form, or webhook.

We had 18 files with “safe” types that looked perfect in the editor.

Then a Stripe webhook sent a field as null instead of string, and the app crashed anyway.

That’s the pitfall: TypeScript can only protect the code it sees.

It cannot trust the outside world.

Zod slowed us down by maybe 30 minutes to add schemas, but it saved a full day of debugging the next time a payload drifted.

TypeScript was still essential.

But for anything crossing a network boundary, Zod won.

My lesson: static types are for your code, runtime validation is for reality.

Which side have you trusted too much, the editor or the data?

This week, the bot got quieter.

I’m building an LLM support assistant for a SaaS team.
It answers customer questions from help docs and past tickets.

Last week, it was fast but sloppy.
This week, I shipped a stricter retrieval layer, which is the part that finds the right source text before the model answers.

That changed one number that mattered:
wrong-answer rate dropped from 18% to 6%.

I used OpenAI for generation and Postgres for stored tickets.
Postgres is our database, the place we keep records.

The win looked clean on a chart.
The messy part was obvious in Slack.

The bot now says “I don’t know” more often.
That sounds worse until you watch support agents.
They trust a cautious answer.
They do not trust a confident guess.

What’s still broken?
Latency jumped from 1.8 seconds to 3.4 seconds on long questions.
The model is waiting on a second search pass, and some replies still miss old tickets with weird wording.

So the lesson this week is simple:
in production, a slower honest answer beats a fast fake one.

What would you ship first: speed, or trust?

I priced work wrong.

I was freelancing on a 6-week client build.
The question was simple: charge by the hour, or charge one fixed price.

That choice changes everything.
Hourly is safer when the scope is blurry.
Fixed price is cleaner when the client wants a clear outcome.

I chose fixed price on purpose.

The tradeoff was real.
If I got fast, I kept the upside.
If I got surprised, I ate the cost.

What most people miss is cash flow.
Hourly can look better on paper.
But clients pay slower when they feel every extra meeting.
A fixed quote with a 50% upfront payment gave me predictability, and predictability mattered more than squeezing every extra dollar.

That mattered because I was solo.
No buffer.
No team to absorb a 2-week delay.

The lesson: pick the pricing model that protects the constraint you actually have, not the one that sounds fairest.

If you’ve freelanced, when did you choose certainty over upside?

One prompt cost $18.

I was building an internal support bot for a fintech team.
It answered employee questions from policy docs, and the bill jumped after launch.

The bot felt smart.
The invoices did not.

We were sending every question to GPT-4o, OpenAI’s general model, plus the full chat history on each turn.
That meant a simple “How do I reset my password?” was carrying 20 previous messages, three pasted policy pages, and a lot of wasted tokens.

Tokens are the chunks of text a model reads.
More chunks means more cost.

I tried caching answers in Redis, a fast memory store.
Helped a little.

I tried shrinking the context window, the amount of text the model sees.
Helped more.

The real problem was upstream.
We had no routing layer, the step that picks the cheapest model for the job.

Password resets did not need GPT-4o.
They needed a smaller model and a clean lookup against the policy index, the searchable doc list.

After that change, our average response dropped from $0.42 to $0.06.
Same bot.
Same users.
Different bill.

The lesson: don’t optimize prompts first.
Optimize what asks the model to think at all.

What’s the most expensive “simple” AI feature you’ve seen ship?

Node.js is not the problem.

The hot take: your team is probably using it badly.

The dominant story is simple: Node.js is for startups, then you "graduate" to something safer when traffic grows.
That sounds neat.
It also made us overcomplicate a service that only needed to stay fast, cheap, and easy to change.

We had one API on Node 18 serving about 2.3 million requests a day.
The latency spikes were real, but the root cause was not Node.
It was 14 different middlewares, too much JSON copying, and a queue that nobody owned.

We trimmed the stack.
Removed 6 middlewares.
Stopped parsing the same payload twice.
Kept the process boring.

p95 latency dropped from 480ms to 140ms in a week.

The strongest argument against this is fair: Node can fall over when you mix heavy CPU work with lots of traffic.
That is true.
If your app is doing image processing or long-running computation, Node is the wrong fit.

But most "Node doesn’t scale" stories I’ve seen were really "our architecture didn’t scale."

The lesson: scale the system before you blame the language.

What’s the real bottleneck you’ve seen blamed on Node?

I trusted the wrong memory.

We were building an AI agent to help our support team answer refund requests.
If it worked, it would save hours a day and keep angry customers from waiting.

The agent kept sounding confident.
It even had access to the right policy docs.

But it still made up details.

The mistake was simple:
I treated the model like a person who could remember the rules.
It isn’t.

An agent is only as good as the instructions, tools, and checks around it.
Without a hard source of truth, it will fill gaps with something that sounds right.

We fixed it by forcing every refund answer through one live policy lookup before the reply.
After that change, the bad answers dropped to almost zero.

The lesson I keep relearning:
don’t ask an agent to remember what it can read.

What’s a place you’ve seen AI sound right and still get it wrong?

We had 18 million rows.
Postgres was slowing down search.

The question was simple:
do we add a GIN index or cache the results in Redis?

GIN indexes in PostgreSQL are built for searching inside data.
Think tags, text, JSON fields, and “find me anything containing this.”

Redis is an in-memory cache.
Think “keep the answer on a sticky note” for fast repeat reads.

We tested both on the same endpoint.

GIN got the query from 420 ms to 38 ms.
Redis got repeated reads to 4 ms.
But Redis also added a second system, cache invalidation, and 2 days of edge cases.

GIN took one migration.
No new service.
No stale data.
No extra code path to debug at 2 a.m.

We picked GIN.

The lesson: if Postgres can answer the question well enough, keep the work inside Postgres.
A second system should earn its place, not just look faster in a benchmark.

What’s the best case you’ve seen for keeping search inside Postgres?

The evals finally caught it.

I’m building an AI support copilot for a small SaaS team.

The goal is simple: help agents answer faster without making up facts.

This week I shipped an eval set of 120 real customer questions, plus a grading script in Python that scores each reply for correctness, tone, and hallucinations.

Before that, we were guessing.

A change looked good in a demo, then failed on the weird edge cases customers actually ask.

After the evals, one model jumped from 68% to 84% on answer quality.

That number sounds good until you see the gap:
it still missed refund policy questions and kept giving confident but wrong account steps.

The broken part is not the model alone.

It’s our eval coverage.

We had plenty of “happy path” questions and almost no angry, messy, half-written tickets from real users.

So the lesson this week was clear:
if you only test the easy cases, your AI will look smart right up until it meets a customer.

What do you think is harder to catch early: wrong answers, or confident wrong answers?

I almost shipped the shortcut.

We were building onboarding for a small SaaS.
Every new user had to connect Stripe, import data, and finish setup fast.

The choice was simple on paper:
build one flexible flow now,
or split it into 3 separate steps.

The flexible version looked cleaner.
One screen, fewer buttons, less code.

But the tradeoff was hidden.
If anything failed, users would lose the whole flow.
And in the first week, 31% of signups were coming from people on weak laptops and shaky Wi‑Fi.

So I chose the boring option.
Three steps.
More clicks.
Less chance of a half-finished setup getting wiped out.

That decision cost us polish.
It saved us from support tickets we would have spent months untangling.

The lesson I keep relearning:
the best architecture is often the one that survives bad internet, tired users, and rushed decisions.

That tradeoff doesn’t apply when the task is truly tiny.
If the whole job takes 30 seconds, friction matters more than failure recovery.

What tradeoff do you think founders ignore most when they’re trying to make a product feel “simple”?

The checkout app crashed Friday.

We were building a payment flow for a small e-commerce client.
Their customers were stuck at the final step, and every failed checkout meant lost orders.

The frontend looked fine.
The API looked fine.
But every few minutes, the whole flow would hang for 30 seconds and then fail.

We blamed Stripe first.
Then the database.
Then the network.

The root cause was boring: one service was doing too much.
The monolith — one big app with shared responsibilities — was handling login, cart updates, payment validation, and receipt emails in the same request path.

A slow email call was blocking the payment response.

We moved receipt sending to a queue — a background line for delayed work — and split payment validation into its own service.
The next deploy cut checkout latency from 8.4 seconds to 1.1 seconds.

What I learned: when one user action depends on too many jobs finishing at once, the whole product feels broken.

Simple architecture is not about being clever.
It is about making the critical path — the steps that must finish now — as short as possible.

Which part of a product do you think is most often doing too much work?

MCP is not the bottleneck.

Everyone keeps talking like the model is brilliant and the missing piece is just better tools.
Or better context.
Or a smarter way to wire everything together.

I built a small support bot with Claude and MCP, the Model Context Protocol that lets an AI call tools in a standard way.
The bot could search tickets, pull orders, and draft replies.

It still sounded lost.

The problem was not access.
It was what I was feeding it, and when.

I spent 2 days shrinking a 14-page context dump into 6 short facts the bot actually needed.
Same tools.
Same model.
Far better answers.

That was the moment context engineering clicked for me:
most AI failures are not “the bot can’t do it.”
They’re “the bot was given the wrong shape of information.”

The strongest counterargument is real: MCP matters because it stops every team from building custom glue for every app.
That is true.
Standard plumbing is useful.

But once the pipes work, the hard part is still deciding what goes through them.

Less context, better chosen, beats more context every time.

What’s the most useful thing you’ve seen an AI ignore because it was buried in too much context?

We let the agent send money alone.

It was a support workflow for refunds.
The stakes were real: if it worked, we could cut response time from hours to minutes.

For 3 days, it looked fine.
Then a customer got the same refund twice.

The bug was not the model.
It was us assuming one good answer meant one safe action.

The agent used OpenAI for reasoning and Stripe for payments.
Stripe is the payment processor.

We had a tool schema — the menu of actions the bot can take — and it had only one rule: “issue refund.”
No idempotency key, which is a unique stamp that prevents duplicate actions.
No human check on money-moving steps.

So when the request retried after a timeout, the second run looked identical.
The bot was “correct.”
The system was not.

We added a simple gate after that:
the agent could draft the refund,
but a person had to approve the final charge.

That single change cut duplicate refunds to zero.

The lesson: an agent is not reliable just because each step sounds reasonable.
It has to be safe when the same step happens twice.

Where have you seen a system look correct on the first pass and still fail in the real world?

React Router won the week.

We needed one UI to let people browse products, open a cart, and keep the browser back button working like a normal website.

The choice was React Router or Next.js App Router, and we had 3 days to ship.

React Router is the classic library for page changes inside React apps.
Next.js App Router is the newer routing system built into Next.js, the React web framework.

React Router gave us freedom, but it also gave us more work.
We had to wire data loading, loading states, and error screens by hand.

Next.js App Router handled those pieces for us.
Server rendering, nested layouts, and route-level loading came built in.

That saved us about 2 days on the first release.
It also cut one ugly bug: a blank screen during slow network calls that had shown up 14 times in testing.

The lesson was simple:
If the app is mostly pages and forms, pick the router that removes work, not the one that gives you the most control.

We shipped with Next.js App Router.

Which side do you trust more when speed matters: more control or fewer moving parts?

I’m building an AI triage tool for support teams.
It helps small teams answer customer tickets faster.

This week I shipped the first inbox import, so a team can pull in 1,247 old tickets and see them in one place.
That mattered because nobody wants to test a support tool on fake data.

The good news: first response time dropped from 14 minutes to 4 minutes in our test queue.
The bad news: 19% of imported threads lost their internal notes.
That means the agent sees the customer story, but not the private context the team used to rely on.

I spent two days blaming the importer.

It turned out the real issue was simpler.
I was treating every message like the same kind of message.
A customer reply, an internal note, and a system event are not the same thing.
If you flatten them too early, you lose the thread.

So the lesson this week was blunt:
shipping fast only helps if you preserve the shape of the real thing.

What’s the hardest part of keeping real-world context intact when you build?

One cache saved us.

We were shipping an LLM feature that answered customer questions inside our app.

The scary part wasn’t the model.
It was the bill and the lag.

Every reply had to feel fast, but every prompt to GPT-4o Mini meant another paid API call.
At 8,000 requests a day, small delays turned into angry users and a very real monthly tab.

The decision was simple to describe:
do we cache full answers, or only cache the “retrieval” step — the part that fetches the source facts before the model writes a reply?

Full-answer caching is cheaper.
It also goes stale fast when product docs change.
Retrieval caching is less dramatic, but it keeps the facts fresh and still cuts most of the work.

We chose retrieval caching.

Why?
Because our support docs changed 3–5 times a week.
A stale answer with a fast response is still a bad answer.

So we cached the facts, not the finished sentence.

The lesson: when you can’t afford to be wrong, cache the part that changes least.

This tradeoff breaks down if your content barely changes, or if you need exact repeatability for compliance.

What would you cache first in an LLM app: the answer, or the facts?
